You are a particle phenomenologist writing a paper section. Your style is data-driven: lead with experimental comparisons, present deviations honestly, use tables extensively.

Write a LaTeX section titled "Mass Relations and Iterative Chains" covering these results:

**1. KOIDE FORMULA**
Q(m₁,m₂,m₃) = (√m₁ + √m₂ + √m₃)² / (m₁ + m₂ + m₃)

For charged leptons (e, μ, τ): Q = 1.500014 ± 0.000015 (uncertainty dominated by δm_τ). Deviation from 3/2 is **0.91σ**.

Exhaustive scan of all C(9,3) = 84 SM triplets: rank 1 is (e,μ,τ) at |Q−3/2| = 1.38×10⁻⁵. Rank 2 is (c,b,t) at 6.2×10⁻³. Gap: 450×.

Look-elsewhere corrected significance: **2.8σ** (Monte Carlo, 10000 random mass sets, log-uniform [0.1, 200000] MeV).

**2. KOIDE ANGLE PARAMETRIZATION**
√m_k = √M₀ (1 + √2 cos(2πk/3 + δ₀))

Lepton fit: M₀ = 313.84 MeV, δ₀ = 2.3166 rad.
δ₀ mod 2π/3 = 0.22223 ≈ 2/9 = 0.22222.
Residual = 7.8×10⁻⁶. This is **35 ppm** relative to 2/9 (NOT 7 ppm as sometimes stated — the denominator matters).
Statistical significance: **3.1σ** look-elsewhere (128 nice fractions p/q with q≤20), **4.3σ** specific to 2/9.

**3. SCALING RULE: M₁ = 3M₀, δ₁ = 3δ₀**

Predictions vs PDG (masses in MeV):
| Quark | Predicted | PDG | Deviation |
|-------|-----------|-----|-----------|
| s | 92.3 | 93.4 | 1.2% |
| c | 1360 | 1270 | 7.1% |
| b | 4197 | 4180 | 0.4% |

Quark Koide: Q(−√s, √c, √b) = 3/2 exactly (residual 2.2×10⁻¹⁶).

NOTE on renormalization: the PDG quark masses are quoted at different scales: m_s(2 GeV), m_c(m_c), m_b(m_b), m_t(pole). The predicted masses are at a single internal scale. Running all to a common scale would change deviations but not the structure.

**4. SECOND SCALING: FAILS**
M₂ = 9M₀, δ₂ = 9δ₀ gives masses (478, 92.3, 16377) MeV. Only the middle mass echoes m_s. No match to any known triplet. The scaling stops at one step. Report as a negative result.

**5. ITERATIVE CHAIN (descending from t, b)**
Step 1: Q(m_b, m_?, m_t) = 3/2 → m_c = 1357 MeV (4 branches total: 1357, 60280, 1.34M, 3.55M MeV)
Step 2: Q(m_c, m_?, m_b) = 3/2 → m_s = 92.17 MeV (negative √m_s emerges naturally)
Step 3: Chain stalls at 0.035 MeV (too light for u, d)
Step 4: Q(m_stall, m_?, m_s) gives m_c = 1357 MeV EXACTLY — cyclic echo at machine epsilon.

The chain is algebraically cyclic: t → c → s → stall → c → ...

**6. CROSS-CHECKS**
- Chain A (descending) and Chain B (lepton-seeded) agree: both give m_s ≈ 92 MeV, m_c ≈ 1357 MeV
- Small discrepancy (1357 vs 1360 MeV) comes from different inputs (PDG t,b vs fitted M₀,δ₀)

Write clean LaTeX with tables. Use \section, \subsection. Include a master comparison table at the end. Be honest about what works and what doesn't.

   121→
   122→The two conditions are:
   123→\begin{enumerate}
   124→\item \textbf{Zero-sum perturbations:} $z_1 + z_2 + z_3 = 0$ 
   125→(definition of $z_0$ as the mean charge).
   126→\item \textbf{Energy balance:} The mean energy of the perturbations 
   127→equals the energy of the unperturbed charge: 
   128→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
   129→\end{enumerate}
   130→
   131→Condition~2 is the physics. It says the splitting scale matches the 
   132→base scale---the coefficient of variation equals unity: 
   133→$\sigma(\zch)/\bar{\zch} = 1$. This is neither perturbative 
   134→($\sigma \ll \bar{\zch}$, giving $Q \to 1/3$, all equal) nor 
   135→dominant ($\sigma \gg \bar{\zch}$, giving $Q \to 1$, one mass 
   136→dominates). The Koide value $2/3$ is the equipartition point.
   137→
   138→\subsection{Why one mass can vanish}
   139→
   140→If $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$, a perturbation $z_k$ can reach 
   141→$|z_k| \sim z_0$, making $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k \approx 0$. A vanishing 
   142→mass is the \emph{natural extreme} of energy balance, not fine-tuning.
   143→
   144→%======================================================================
   145→\section{Seed Triples: The $(0, a, b)$ Structure}
   146→\label{sec:seeds}
   147→%======================================================================
   148→
   149→\subsection{Analytical condition}
   150→
   151→For a triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ with one massless member, the 
   152→Koide ratio becomes:
   153→\begin{equation}
   154→Q = \frac{\zch_a^2 + \zch_b^2}{(\zch_a + \zch_b)^2}\,.
   155→\end{equation}
   156→Setting $Q = 2/3$ and writing $r = \zch_a/\zch_b$ (with $r < 1$):
   157→\begin{equation}
   158→3(r^2 + 1) = 2(r + 1)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad r^2 - 4r + 1 = 0\,,
   159→\end{equation}
   160→giving:
   161→\begin{equation}
   162→\boxed{r = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.}
   163→\end{equation}
   164→
   165→Equivalently, in mass space: 
   166→$m_{\text{light}}/m_{\text{heavy}} = (2 - \sqrt{3})^2 = 7 - 4\sqrt{3} 
   167→\approx 0.07180$.
   168→
   169→This is a \emph{universal} prediction: any Koide triple with one 
   170→massless member has the same charge ratio between the two massive 
   171→members, regardless of their absolute scale.
   172→
   173→\subsection{The quark seed: $(0, s, c)$}
   174→
   175→The strange and charm quark charges give:\footnote{Throughout 
   176→this paper, quark masses follow PDG conventions: 
   177→$m_s(2\GeV) = 93.4\MeV$, $m_c(m_c) = 1.27\GeV$, 
   178→$m_b(m_b) = 4.18\GeV$, and the top pole mass 
   179→$m_t = 172.76\GeV$. This is a mixed convention: the light 
   180→quark is evaluated at 2 GeV while heavier quarks are 
   181→evaluated at their own scale. Since QCD mass ratios are 
   182→RG invariants (the anomalous dimension $\gamma_m$ is 
   183→flavor-universal at leading order), the Koide ratio $Q$ is 
   184→scheme-independent. However, the seed ratio 
   185→$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ as computed here uses masses at 
   186→\emph{different} scales, giving $0.271$; at a common scale 
   187→the ratio is $\sim 0.29$, further from $2 - \sqrt{3} = 0.268$. 
   188→The meson seed, using unambiguous physical (pole) masses, 
   189→is free of this scheme issue and provides the cleaner test.}
   190→\begin{equation}
   191→\frac{\sqrt{m_s}}{\sqrt{m_c}} = \frac{9.67}{35.64} = 0.2712\,, 
   192→\qquad r_{\text{Koide}} = 0.2679\,, \qquad \text{deviation: } 1.2\%\,.
   193→\end{equation}
   194→
   195→This triple has $Q = 0.6644$, deviating from $2/3$ by only $0.35\%$. 
   196→Its vacuum energy is $v_0^2 = (\zch_s + \zch_c)^2/9 \approx 228\MeV$.
   197→
   198→A historical note: Harari, Haut, and Weyers \cite{Harari} observed 
   199→a quark mass ratio pattern that, in the language of the present paper, 
   200→corresponds to an approximate seed triple---$(0, d, s)$---at a time 
   201→when the up quark mass was 
   202→still thought to be possibly zero \cite{Harari}. With current masses, 
   203→$\sqrt{m_d}/\sqrt{m_s} = 0.224$, deviating from $2 - \sqrt{3}$ by 
   204→$16\%$---too large for a good Koide triple. But if $m_s$ were 
   205→$\sim 150\MeV$ and $m_d \sim 7\MeV$ (values common in that era), the 
   206→ratio improves considerably. The seed idea was already present, ahead 
   207→of its time.
   208→
   209→\subsection{The meson seed: $(0, \pi, D_s)$}
   210→
   211→The pion and $D_s$ meson charges give:
   212→\begin{equation}
   213→\frac{\sqrt{m_\pi}}{\sqrt{m_{D_s}}} = \frac{11.81}{44.37} = 0.2663\,, 
   214→\qquad r_{\text{Koide}} = 0.2679\,, \qquad \text{deviation: } 0.6\%\,.
   215→\end{equation}
   216→
   217→This gives $Q = 0.6679$, deviating by $0.18\%$. It is the 
   218→\textbf{meson counterpart} of the quark seed $(0, s, c)$---both 
   219→involve the strange-charm sector. Indeed, $D_s = c\bar{s}$ is the 
   220→meson that directly contains the $s$ and $c$ quarks.
   221→
   222→\subsection{The lepton triple as an ``almost-seed''}
   223→
   224→The charged lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ has $\zch_e = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}$, 
   225→which is small but nonzero. If $m_e$ were zero, we would need 
   226→$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 2 - \sqrt{3} = 0.2679$; the actual value 
   227→is $0.2439$, deviating by $9\%$. So the leptons are \emph{not} an 
   228→exact seed---the electron, while very light, is not massless. But the 
   229→triple is clearly the same family: the near-critical Koide phase puts 
   230→one member close to zero.
   231→
   232→\subsection{Vacuum energy coincidence}
   233→
   234→The $v_0^2$ values of the seed triples cluster remarkably:
   235→
   236→\begin{center}
   237→\begin{tabular}{lcc}
   238→\toprule
   239→Triple & $v_0^2$ (MeV) & $v_0$ ($\MeV^{1/2}$) \\
   240→\midrule
   241→$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 314 & 17.72 \\
   242→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 351 & 18.73 \\
   243→$(0, s, c)$ & 228 & 15.10 \\
   244→\bottomrule
   245→\end{tabular}
   246→\end{center}
   247→
   248→The lepton and meson seed triples share a vacuum energy around 
   249→310--350~MeV, squarely in the QCD scale range. This is the 
   250→sBootstrap connection made quantitative: \textbf{the lepton triple 
   251→and the meson seed triple operate at the same scale.}
   252→
   253→%======================================================================
   254→\section{A Tale of Two Tuples}
   255→\label{sec:two_tuples}
   256→%======================================================================
   257→
   258→\subsection{From seed to full triple}
   259→
   260→The seed triples grow into full triples upon symmetry breaking:
   261→
   262→\begin{center}
   263→\begin{tabular}{lccc}
   264→\toprule
   265→Sector & Seed triple & Full triple & $v_0^2$ (full) \\
   266→\midrule
   267→Quarks & $(0, s, c)$ & $(-s, c, b)$ & 913 MeV \\
   268→Mesons & $(0, \pi, D_s)$ & $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & 1230 MeV \\
   269→Leptons & $(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & $(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 314 MeV \\
   270→\bottomrule
   271→\end{tabular}
   272→\end{center}
   273→
   274→In each case:
   275→\begin{itemize}
   276→\item The seed has a massless (or near-massless) member, two massive 
   277→members in the ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$, and $Q \approx 2/3$.
   278→\item The full triple has \textbf{no} massless member: the zero has 
   279→been lifted (to $m_e$, or the sign-flipped strange/pion mass). The 
   280→$b$ quark and $B$ meson are always present as flavor degrees of 
   281→freedom; what the breaking does is give them their \emph{distinctive 
   282→masses}---the specific values that complete the Koide triple. Before 
   283→breaking, the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$); 
   284→after, it acquires $\zch_b = 64.65\MeV^{1/2}$, the value selected 
   285→by the energy balance with $c$ and the sign-flipped $s$. The bloom 
   286→is not particle creation but \emph{mass generation}.
   287→\end{itemize}
   288→
   289→The ``tale of two tuples'' is this: \textbf{each sector presents 
   290→first as a seed with a massless member, then ``blooms'' into a 
   291→full triple when SUSY breaks.} The massless member is the necessary 
   292→initial condition; the breaking lifts it and generates the full spectrum.
   293→
   294→\subsection{Precision summary}
   295→
   296→\begin{center}
   297→\begin{tabular}{lccc}
   298→\toprule
   299→Triple & $Q$ & Dev.\ from $2/3$ & $v_0^2$ (MeV) \\
   300→\midrule
   301→\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textit{Seed triples}} \\
   302→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.6679 & 0.18\% & 351 \\
   303→$(0, s, c)$ & 0.6644 & 0.35\% & 228 \\
   304→$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.6667 & 0.001\% & 314 \\
   305→\midrule
   306→\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textit{Full triples}} \\
   307→$(e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.66666 & 0.001\% & 314 \\
   308→$(-\pi, D_s, B)$ & 0.6674 & 0.10\% & 1230 \\
   309→$(-s, c, b)$ & 0.6750 & 1.24\% & 913 \\
   310→$(c, b, t)$ & 0.6695 & 0.42\% & 29\,580 \\
   311→\bottomrule
   312→\end{tabular}
   313→\end{center}
   314→
   315→The lepton triple is unique: seed and full triple are nearly 
   316→identical, because the electron mass is small enough to serve as 
   317→the ``zero'' while being nonzero enough to make $Q$ extremely 
   318→precise.
   319→

319→
   320→\subsection{Orthogonality of seed and full triples}
   321→
   322→Each Koide triple defines a perturbation vector 
   323→$\vec{z} = (z_1, z_2, z_3)$ constrained to the plane $\sum z_k = 0$ 
   324→with length $|\vec{z}|^2 = 3z_0^2$. The angle between seed and 
   325→full triples measures how much the breaking rotates the spectrum 
   326→in charge space:
   327→
   328→\begin{center}
   329→\begin{tabular}{lc}
   330→\toprule
   331→Pair of triples & Angle \\
   332→\midrule
   333→$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(-s,c,b)$ & $22^\circ$ \\
   334→$(0,\pi,D_s)$ vs.\ $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ & $26^\circ$ \\
   335→$(e,\mu,\tau)$ vs.\ $(0,\mu,\tau)$ & $0.8^\circ$ \\
   336→$(0,s,c)$ vs.\ $(0,\pi,D_s)$ & $0.3^\circ$ \\
   337→\bottomrule
   338→\end{tabular}
   339→\end{center}
   340→
   341→Two results are notable. First, the quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson 
   342→seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ are \textbf{nearly parallel} ($0.3^\circ$): the 
   343→meson seed is the composite image of the quark seed, with the GOR 
   344→map preserving direction while changing scale. Second, the lepton 
   345→triple is essentially its own seed ($0.8^\circ$)---the bloom has 
   346→barely begun.
   347→
   348→
   349→%======================================================================
   350→\section{The Meson Koide Triple: $(-\pi, D_s, B)$}
   351→\label{sec:mesonkoide}
   352→%======================================================================
   353→
   354→With the pion charge negative, the triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ satisfies:
   355→\begin{equation}
   356→Q(-\pi, D_s, B) = 
   357→\frac{m_\pi + m_{D_s} + m_B}{(-\sqrt{m_\pi} + \sqrt{m_{D_s}} + \sqrt{m_B})^2}
   358→= 0.6674\,,
   359→\end{equation}
   360→deviating from $2/3$ by $0.10\%$---the second most precise Koide 
   361→triple known. The energy balance:
   362→\begin{equation}
   363→\frac{\langle z_k^2\rangle}{z_0^2} = 1.002\,.
   364→\end{equation}
   365→
   366→For comparison, the ``standard'' all-positive quark triples $(d,s,b)$ 
   367→and $(u,c,t)$ deviate from $Q = 2/3$ by 9.7\% and 27\% respectively 
   368→at $\overline{\text{MS}}$ masses. \textbf{The meson triple is more 
   369→precise than any quark triple} except $(c,b,t)$.
   370→
   371→The pion's negative charge has physical meaning: as a pseudo-Goldstone 
   372→boson, the pion would be massless ($\zch = 0$) if chiral symmetry were 
   373→exact. It sits just below zero ($\zch_\pi = -11.81$), approaching zero 
   374→from the negative side as $m_q \to 0$. The sign of $\zch$ encodes 
   375→Goldstone nature: pseudo-Goldstone bosons sit on the negative side 
   376→of the critical point, having crossed through zero from positive 
   377→territory during chiral symmetry breaking.
   378→
   379→%======================================================================
   380→\section{The Third Tuple: $(c, b, t)$}
   381→\label{sec:cbt}
   382→%======================================================================
   383→
   384→\subsection{A tuple without mesons}
   385→
   386→The triple $(c, b, t)$ with all positive signs gives $Q = 0.6695$, 
   387→deviating by $0.42\%$. Its vacuum energy $v_0^2 \approx 29.6\GeV$ 
   388→is far above the QCD scale.
   389→
   390→This triple has \textbf{no meson counterpart}: the top quark decays 
   391→weakly ($t \to Wb$, lifetime $\sim 5 \times 10^{-25}$~s) before the 
   392→strong interaction can bind it ($\tau_{\text{QCD}} \sim 1/\Lambda_{\text{QCD}} 
   393→\sim 10^{-24}$~s). No $T$-meson ($t\bar{q}$) exists. The meson matrix 
   394→$M^i{}_j$ extends only to $b$-flavored states; the top sector lives 
   395→purely at the quark level.
   396→
   397→\subsection{Diquarks and the top}
   398→
   399→In the sBootstrap, diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ are the scalar partners of 
   400→antiquarks $\bar{q}_k$, with the baryon acting as the SUSY generator 
   401→\cite{CattoGursey, Miyazawa}. For five active flavors 
   402→$(u, d, s, c, b)$, the diquarks form a representation of $SU(5)_f$.
   403→
   404→The top diquarks $[tq]$ are kinematic objects only---they do not 
   405→hadronize. Yet their masses should follow the same pattern as other 
   406→diquarks: \textbf{there is no dynamical reason for top diquark masses 
   407→to differ from the others}. The $(c,b,t)$ Koide triple governs the 
   408→quark masses that enter the diquark sector, and its energy balance 
   409→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ constrains the possible diquark 
   410→spectrum even though the top diquarks are never observed as bound 
   411→states.
   412→
   413→\subsection{The $(c,b,t)$ triple has no seed}
   414→
   415→Unlike the $(0,s,c)$ and $(0,\pi,D_s)$ seeds, there is no obvious 
   416→$(0, x, y)$ seed for the $(c,b,t)$ triple. No pair of quarks in this 
   417→sector satisfies $\sqrt{m_{\text{light}}}/\sqrt{m_{\text{heavy}}} 
   418→\approx 2 - \sqrt{3}$: one has $\sqrt{m_c}/\sqrt{m_t} = 0.086$ 
   419→(too small) and $\sqrt{m_c}/\sqrt{m_b} = 0.551$ (too large).
   420→
   421→This means $(c,b,t)$ is \emph{not} a ``bloomed seed'' in the same 
   422→sense as $(-s,c,b)$. It may represent a fundamentally different 
   423→structure---a triple that was never seeded by a massless member but 
   424→instead emerged fully formed at a higher breaking scale, perhaps 
   425→connected to electroweak symmetry breaking rather than to chiral 
   426→symmetry breaking.
   427→
   428→%======================================================================
   429→\section{The Diquark Sector and SU(5) Flavor}
   430→\label{sec:diquarks}
   431→%======================================================================
   432→
   433→\subsection{Counting partners}
   434→
   435→In the full sBootstrap \cite{Rivero2005}, the scalar partners of 
   436→the Standard Model fermions include both mesons ($q\bar{q}$) and 
   437→diquarks ($qq$). For five active flavors, the meson matrix gives 
   438→$5 \times 5 = 25$ scalars. The diquarks $[q_i q_j]$ form a 
   439→representation of $SU(5)_f$:
   440→\begin{equation}
   441→\mathbf{5} \otimes \mathbf{5} = \overline{\mathbf{10}}_A 
   442→\oplus \mathbf{15}_S\,.
   443→\end{equation}
   444→
   445→The symmetric $\mathbf{15}_S$ gives 15 diquarks including 
   446→same-flavor states $[uu]$, $[dd]$, \ldots, while the antisymmetric 
   447→$\overline{\mathbf{10}}_A$ gives 10 diquarks with $i \neq j$ only.
   448→
   449→\subsection{The symmetric representation}
   450→\label{subsec:sym15}
   451→
   452→The sBootstrap SUSY generator acts asymmetrically on the two 
   453→fermion sectors. When it acts on a \emph{lepton}, it produces a 
   454→meson: $Q \cdot \ell \sim q\bar{q}$. Since the quark and antiquark 
   455→are distinguishable, the meson lies in the full 
   456→$\mathbf{5} \otimes \bar{\mathbf{5}} = \mathbf{24} + \mathbf{1}$. 
   457→There is no Pauli constraint, and the meson spectrum is perfectly 
   458→reproduced.
   459→
   460→When it acts on a \emph{quark}, it produces a diquark: 
   461→$Q \cdot q_i \sim q_i q_j$. The bosonic SUSY generator applied to 
   462→a single flavor index $i$ naturally produces the \textbf{symmetric} 
   463→product---the $\mathbf{15}_S$ representation, not the antisymmetric 
   464→$\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. This is the representation identified 
   465→in the $SO(32)$ decomposition \cite{Rivero2005}: the adjoint of 
   466→$SO(32)$ under $SU(5)_f$ decomposes to include the 
   467→$\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ (diquarks) alongside the 
   468→$\mathbf{1} + \mathbf{24}$ (mesons).
   469→
   470→The 15 includes 5 same-flavor states ($[uu]$, $[dd]$, $[ss]$, 
   471→$[cc]$, $[bb]$) absent from the $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. Among 
   472→these are exotic $+4/3$-charged diquarks such as $[uu]$. The cost: 
   473→an additional chiral $+4/3$ quark per generation, as identified 
   474→in \cite{Rivero2005}. Whether these exotic states appear in nature 
   475→as resonances, or whether they decouple at some high scale, is an 
   476→open question.
   477→
   478→\subsection{The Pauli theorem for scalar diquarks}
   479→\label{subsec:pauli}
   480→
   481→The requirement of the symmetric $\mathbf{15}$ conflicts with 
   482→Fermi statistics---not merely for $L=0$ diquarks, but for 
   483→\emph{all} scalar diquarks in the color $\bar{3}$ channel, 
   484→regardless of internal orbital structure.
   485→
   486→\medskip
   487→\noindent\textbf{Theorem.}
   488→\textit{For a spin-0 ($J=0$) diquark in the color $\bar{3}$ 
   489→(antisymmetric) representation, the flavor wave function is 
   490→antisymmetric for \textbf{all} values of the orbital angular 
   491→momentum $L$ and spin $S$.}
   492→
   493→\medskip
   494→\noindent\textit{Proof.}
   495→$J = 0$ requires $L = S$ (since $|L - S| \leq J \leq L + S$). 
   496→The exchange symmetry of the spin-orbital wave function is 
   497→$(-1)^{L+S+1} = (-1)^{2L+1} = -1$ for all $L$. Combined with 
   498→the color $\bar{3}$ factor $(-1)$, the non-flavor product is 
   499→$(-1)(-1) = +1$ (symmetric). Total antisymmetry then requires 
   500→flavor to be antisymmetric: $\overline{\mathbf{10}}$. \hfill$\square$
   501→
   502→\medskip
   503→This result is purely kinematic---independent of dynamics, 
   504→binding mechanisms, or spatial extent. No wave function, however 
   505→extended, can produce a symmetric-flavor scalar diquark in color 
   506→$\bar{3}$ while respecting Fermi statistics. The key cases:
   507→
   508→\begin{center}
   509→\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
   510→\toprule
   511→$L$ & $S$ & Spin-orbital $(-1)^{2L+1}$ & $\times$ Color & Flavor \\
   512→\midrule
   513→0 & 0 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
   514→1 & 1 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
   515→2 & 2 & $-1$ & $\times\,(-1) = +1$ & must be A \\
   516→\bottomrule
   517→\end{tabular}
   518→\end{center}

1298→\section{Open Problems}
  1299→\label{sec:open}
  1300→%======================================================================
  1301→
  1302→\subsection{The diquark sector}
  1303→The Pauli theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) proves that no $J=0$, 
  1304→color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor, closing all 
  1305→field-theoretic escape routes ($L=0$, P-wave, or higher). The 
  1306→sBootstrap's $\mathbf{15}$ diquark must therefore be non-composite: 
  1307→either an algebraic image of the Miyazawa supercharge or a 
  1308→fundamental $SO(32)$ string state. The exotic $+4/3$-charged 
  1309→diquarks predicted by the $\mathbf{15}$ are a testable consequence.
  1310→
  1311→\subsection{What determines the Koide phase?}
  1312→The energy balance constrains but does not uniquely fix $\delta$. 
  1313→The value $\delta_\ell \approx 2/9$ for leptons must come from 
  1314→dynamics. Is it a fixed point?
  1315→
  1316→\subsection{Connecting the three tuples}
  1317→The light sector (seed $\to$ bloom, scale $\sim \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$) 
  1318→and heavy sector ($c,b,t$, scale $\sim v_{\text{EW}}$) must connect. 
  1319→The charm and bottom quarks appear in both, providing the links.
  1320→
  1321→\subsection{The baryon sector}
  1322→In Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, baryons are part of the 
  1323→low-energy spectrum. Their SUSY partners are ``baryonic fermions'' 
  1324→with no known candidates among observed particles.
  1325→
  1326→\subsection{The strong CP problem}
  1327→The principle ``Nature abhors chirality'' predicts $\bar{\theta} = 0$ 
  1328→but provides no mechanism.
  1329→
  1330→\subsection{The kaon--muon puzzle}
  1331→The SUSY splitting $m_K^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx (482\MeV)^2$ is much 
  1332→larger than $f_K^2 \approx (110\MeV)^2$, showing that the breaking 
  1333→is strongly flavor-dependent.
  1334→
  1335→%======================================================================
  1336→\section{Conclusions}
  1337→\label{sec:conclusions}
  1338→%======================================================================
  1339→
  1340→Three principal results:
  1341→
  1342→\paragraph{1.\ The Koide formula is energy equipartition.}
  1343→$Q = 2/3$ is $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$: perturbation energies 
  1344→match the vacuum energy. The signed-charge framework resolves the 
  1345→electron mass puzzle (near-cancellation, not fine-tuning) and reveals 
  1346→that mesons satisfy Koide with $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ to $0.1\%$.
  1347→
  1348→\paragraph{2.\ Seed triples set the stage for SUSY breaking.}
  1349→The quark seed $(0,s,c)$ and meson seed $(0,\pi,D_s)$ share the 
  1350→universal ratio $2 - \sqrt{3}$ and operate at $v_0^2 \sim 
  1351→230$--$350\MeV$, close to the lepton triple's $v_0^2 = 314\MeV$. 
  1352→The massless member is the necessary condition for F-term 
  1353→(O'Raifeartaigh) SUSY breaking. Upon breaking, seeds bloom into 
  1354→full triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi,D_s,B)$. The lepton triple 
  1355→is the ``almost-seed'' that has barely bloomed.
  1356→
  1357→\paragraph{3.\ Two breaking mechanisms plus a heavy-sector origin.}
  1358→F-term breaking (via the massless seed member) generates the light 
  1359→sector; the Volkov--Akulov relation $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is its 
  1360→low-energy realization, not a separate mechanism. D-term breaking 
  1361→($U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI parameter) provides electromagnetic 
  1362→splittings as a perturbative correction. The heavy triple 
  1363→$(c,b,t)$ stands apart: no seed, no meson shadow, scale far above 
  1364→$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, possibly connected to electroweak breaking.
  1365→
  1366→The diquark sector, required to complete the SUSY multiplet count, 
  1367→fills the symmetric $\mathbf{15} + \overline{\mathbf{15}}$ of 
  1368→$SU(5)_f$---the representation produced naturally by the SUSY 
  1369→generator acting on quarks and by the $SO(32)$ decomposition. A 
  1370→kinematic theorem (\S\ref{subsec:pauli}) shows that \emph{no} 
  1371→$J=0$ color-$\bar{3}$ diquark can have symmetric flavor, regardless 
  1372→of orbital angular momentum: the sBootstrap diquark in the 
  1373→$\mathbf{15}$ cannot be a $qq$ composite. It must be an 
  1374→algebraically generated object (Miyazawa supercharge) or a 
  1375→fundamental string-theoretic state ($SO(32)$ adjoint).
  1376→
  1377→The organizing principle is that each sector of the spectrum begins 
  1378→with a massless member (a seed), which is the necessary condition 
  1379→for spontaneous SUSY breaking. The breaking lifts the zero, 
  1380→generates the full triple, and the energy-balance condition 
  1381→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is preserved as a fixed point 
  1382→of the dynamics. The entire charged fermion and pseudoscalar meson 
  1383→spectrum is then determined by a small number of parameters: the 
  1384→vacuum charges $v_0$ of the three tuples, the Koide phases 
  1385→$\delta$ that control the bloom, and the SUSY-breaking scale 
  1386→$f_\pi$ that separates bosons from fermions.
  1387→

568→\section{SUSY Breaking: From Seeds to Spectrum}
   569→\label{sec:breaking}
   570→%======================================================================
   571→
   572→The SUSY-breaking story begins with the foundational observation 
   573→\eqref{eq:pimu}: $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 \approx f_\pi^2$. This is the 
   574→Volkov--Akulov mass formula for a pseudo-Goldstino---the muon---whose 
   575→superpartner---the pion---is a pseudo-Goldstone boson. The SUSY-breaking 
   576→scale is $f_\pi \approx 92\MeV$, and the breaking mechanism is chiral: 
   577→$\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry and the sBootstrap SUSY 
   578→simultaneously. The Volkov--Akulov realization is the \emph{physical 
   579→starting point}; what follows in this section is the SQCD machinery 
   580→that makes it precise.
   581→
   582→The dynamics must accomplish three things: generate the seed-to-bloom 
   583→transition for each tuple, preserve the energy-balance condition 
   584→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ through the breaking, and produce 
   585→the correct splitting between meson and lepton masses at scale $f_\pi$.
   586→
   587→\subsection{The Seiberg framework}
   588→
   589→The natural arena is $\mathcal{N}=1$ SQCD with gauge group $SU(3)_c$ 
   590→and $N_f$ flavors of chiral superfields $Q^i$, $\bar{Q}_j$ in the 
   591→fundamental and antifundamental representations. For $N_f = N_c = 3$ 
   592→(the case relevant to light QCD), Seiberg's exact results 
   593→\cite{Seiberg1995} establish that the theory confines, with the 
   594→low-energy degrees of freedom being the composite meson superfield
   595→\begin{equation}
   596→M^i{}_j = Q^i \cdot \bar{Q}_j\,,
   597→\end{equation}
   598→a $3\times 3$ matrix of chiral superfields, together with baryons 
   599→$B = \epsilon_{ijk}Q^iQ^jQ^k$ and $\bar{B}$. These satisfy the 
   600→quantum-modified constraint
   601→\begin{equation}
   602→\label{eq:seiberg}
   603→\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^{2N_c}\,,
   604→\end{equation}
   605→where $\Lambda$ is the dynamical scale.
   606→
   607→Each entry $M^i{}_j$ is a chiral superfield containing a complex 
   608→scalar (the meson) and a Weyl fermion (the lepton, in the sBootstrap 
   609→identification). The scalar and fermion are degenerate in the SUSY 
   610→limit; SUSY breaking splits them.
   611→
   612→\subsection{The three chiral superfields of each seed}
   613→
   614→The seed triple $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ maps naturally onto three 
   615→chiral superfields with the following mass structure before breaking:
   616→
   617→\begin{center}
   618→\begin{tabular}{lcl}
   619→\toprule
   620→Superfield & Mass & Role \\
   621→\midrule
   622→$\Phi_0$ & $m_0 = 0$ & Massless: flat direction \\
   623→$\Phi_a$ & $m_a = \zch_a^2$ & Light massive member \\
   624→$\Phi_b$ & $m_b = \zch_b^2$ & Heavy massive member \\
   625→\bottomrule
   626→\end{tabular}
   627→\end{center}
   628→
   629→The ratio $\zch_a/\zch_b = 2 - \sqrt{3}$ is enforced by the 
   630→Koide energy-balance condition applied to the seed.
   631→
   632→In the Seiberg description, these three superfields are entries of 
   633→the meson matrix $M^i{}_j$. For the $(0, s, c)$ seed, the relevant 
   634→entries involve the strange and charm quarks; $\Phi_0$ is the entry 
   635→whose quark-mass combination vanishes in the seed limit (i.e., the 
   636→direction in flavor space where $m_i + m_j \to 0$ through GOR).
   637→
   638→\subsection{F-term breaking: the O'Raifeartaigh mechanism}
   639→\label{subsec:fterm}
   640→
   641→The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} tells us \emph{that} SUSY is broken at 
   642→scale $f_\pi$. To understand \emph{how}, we need the microscopic 
   643→mechanism. The O'Raifeartaigh model \cite{ORaifeartaigh} is the simplest 
   644→realization of spontaneous $F$-term SUSY breaking. With three chiral 
   645→superfields, the superpotential
   646→\begin{equation}
   647→W = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_1\Phi_2 + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_1^2
   648→\end{equation}
   649→gives $F$-terms:
   650→\begin{align}
   651→F_{\Phi_0}^* &= f + g\,\phi_1^2\,, \\
   652→F_{\Phi_1}^* &= m\,\phi_2 + 2g\,\phi_0\phi_1\,, \\
   653→F_{\Phi_2}^* &= m\,\phi_1\,.
   654→\end{align}
   655→If $f \neq 0$ and $m \neq 0$, then $F_{\Phi_2}^* = 0$ requires 
   656→$\phi_1 = 0$, but then $F_{\Phi_0}^* = f \neq 0$: SUSY is 
   657→\textbf{necessarily broken}. The key features are:
   658→\begin{enumerate}
   659→\item A massless field ($\Phi_0$) exists at tree level---it is the 
   660→pseudo-modulus.
   661→\item The breaking is triggered because $F_{\Phi_0}$ cannot vanish.
   662→\item After including loop corrections, $\Phi_0$ acquires a mass 
   663→(the Coleman--Weinberg potential lifts the flat direction).
   664→\end{enumerate}
   665→
   666→The structural parallel with the seed triples is:
   667→
   668→\begin{center}
   669→\begin{tabular}{llll}
   670→\toprule
   671→O'Raifeartaigh & Flavor & Seed & After breaking \\
   672→\midrule
   673→$\Phi_0$ (massless) & $b$ / $B$ & $\zch = 0$ (flat direction) & $\zch_b = 64.65$ (mass generated) \\
   674→$\Phi_1$ (light) & $s$ / $\pi$ & $\zch_s = 9.67$ & $\zch \to -9.67$ (sign-flipped) \\
   675→$\Phi_2$ (heavy) & $c$ / $D_s$ & $\zch_c = 35.64$ & $\zch_c \approx 35.64$ (shifted) \\
   676→\midrule
   677→ & & $f = 0$ (SUSY) & $f = f_\pi$ (broken) \\
   678→\bottomrule
   679→\end{tabular}
   680→\end{center}
   681→
   682→This parallel is \emph{structural}, not quantitative: the minimal 
   683→O'Raifeartaigh model gives $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ the same mass 
   684→parameter $m$, whereas the seed triple has a mass ratio 
   685→$m_s/m_c = (2 - \sqrt{3})^2 \approx 0.07$---far from degenerate. 
   686→The hierarchy between the two massive members must come from 
   687→additional dynamics beyond the minimal O'Raifeartaigh superpotential. 
   688→What the O'Raifeartaigh structure \emph{does} provide is the essential 
   689→feature: a massless flat direction ($\Phi_0 = $ the $b$-direction) 
   690→whose $F$-term is forced nonzero. The $b$ quark and $B$ meson are 
   691→always present as flavor degrees of freedom; the seed configuration 
   692→$(0, s, c)$ means the $b$-direction sits at the flat direction before 
   693→breaking. The bloom is not particle creation but mass generation: 
   694→breaking lifts $\Phi_0$ from zero to $\zch_b \neq 0$, simultaneously 
   695→flipping the sign of $\zch_s$, so that the same three flavors 
   696→rearrange from $(0, s, c)$ into $(-s, c, b)$.
   697→
   698→A striking difference from standard O'Raifeartaigh: the 
   699→pseudo-modulus $\Phi_0$ normally acquires the \emph{smallest} mass 
   700→in the spectrum (a loop-suppressed Coleman--Weinberg mass, 
   701→parametrically $\sim g^2 f/16\pi^2 m$). In the sBootstrap bloom, 
   702→the $b$-direction acquires the \emph{largest} mass 
   703→($m_b \gg m_s, m_c$). The bloom is far more dramatic than a 
   704→perturbative CW lifting---it is a nonperturbative, strong-dynamics 
   705→effect in which confinement generates a mass of order $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/m_q$, 
   706→not $g^2/(16\pi^2)$. This inversion is a signature of the 
   707→dynamical (SQCD) realization versus the perturbative 
   708→(superpotential) one.
   709→
   710→The identification $f = \langle F_{\Phi_0}\rangle \sim f_\pi$ is the 
   711→sBootstrap's central claim: the SUSY-breaking $F$-term is the chiral 
   712→condensate parameter.
   713→
   714→In SQCD, this is realized dynamically rather than through a 
   715→superpotential: the chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ acts as the 
   716→nonzero $F$-term, the impossibility of restoring chiral symmetry 
   717→in the QCD vacuum is the dynamical analogue of $f \neq 0$, and the 
   718→pion---the would-be Goldstone boson that acquires mass from explicit 
   719→chiral breaking---plays the role of $\Phi_0$ whose mass is lifted 
   720→by loop corrections (here, by quark masses).
   721→
   722→\subsection{The Goldstino direction and the Volkov--Akulov realization}
   723→\label{subsec:va}
   724→
   725→When SUSY is spontaneously broken, a massless fermion appears: the 
   726→Goldstino, the fermionic analogue of the Goldstone boson. Its decay 
   727→constant $f_{\text{VA}}$ sets the SUSY-breaking scale through the 
   728→Volkov--Akulov Lagrangian \cite{VolkovAkulov1973}:
   729→\begin{equation}
   730→\mathcal{L}_{\text{VA}} = -f_{\text{VA}}^2 \det\!\left(
   731→\delta_\mu^\nu + \frac{i}{f_{\text{VA}}^2}\,\bar{\lambda}\bar{\sigma}^\nu
   732→\partial_\mu\lambda\right),
   733→\end{equation}
   734→where $\lambda$ is the Goldstino field. At low energies, the Goldstino 
   735→couples derivatively with strength $\sim 1/f_{\text{VA}}^2$, exactly 
   736→as the pion couples with strength $\sim 1/f_\pi^2$ in the chiral 
   737→Lagrangian.
   738→
   739→The sBootstrap identification is:
   740→\begin{equation}
   741→\label{eq:va_id}
   742→f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi\,, \qquad 
   743→\text{Goldstino} = \text{muon (partner of }\pi\text{)}\,.
   744→\end{equation}
   745→More precisely, the muon is a \emph{pseudo-Goldstino}: in the 
   746→limit $m_q \to 0$ where the pion becomes exactly massless (a true 
   747→Goldstone boson), its fermionic partner also becomes massless (a 
   748→true Goldstino). The finite quark masses explicitly break both 
   749→chiral symmetry (giving $m_\pi \neq 0$) and the sBootstrap SUSY 
   750→(giving $m_\mu \neq m_\pi$), with the splitting controlled by 
   751→$f_\pi$:
   752→\begin{equation}
   753→m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2\,.
   754→\end{equation}
   755→
   756→This is the Volkov--Akulov relation for the pseudo-Goldstino: the 
   757→mass splitting between the scalar (pion) and its fermionic partner 
   758→(muon) is set by the SUSY-breaking scale squared.
   759→
   760→The VA mechanism is not a separate breaking from the F-term: it is 
   761→the \emph{low-energy, composite-level description} of the same 
   762→breaking. At the microscopic level, $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ 
   763→in the meson superfield; at the composite level, this manifests 
   764→as the Goldstino coupling with $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$. These 
   765→two quantities---the Goldstino decay constant and the pion decay 
   766→constant---are \emph{identified} in the sBootstrap; this is the 
   767→central claim, not a derivation.
   768→
   769→The identification should not be confused with the standard relation 
   770→between the $F$-term VEV and the chiral condensate. In standard 
   771→SQCD, $\langle F\rangle \sim |\qqbar|/f_\pi \sim (250\MeV)^3/92\MeV 
   772→\approx 170{,}000\MeV^2$, while $f_\pi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2$---a 
   773→factor of $\sim 20$ discrepancy. The GOR relation 
   774→$m_\pi^2 f_\pi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$ connects these through the 
   775→small quark masses, but does not equate them. The sBootstrap 
   776→identification $f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$ is therefore a 
   777→\emph{low-energy effective} statement about the composite sector, 
   778→not a microscopic identity. It says: the scale at which SUSY 
   779→appears broken in the meson--lepton spectrum is $f_\pi$, regardless 
   780→of the microscopic $F$-term that produces this breaking.
   781→
   782→The VA realization gives more than a mass formula---it organizes the 
   783→entire breaking narrative. In the chiral limit ($m_q \to 0$), both 
   784→pion and muon are exactly massless: the pion as the Goldstone boson 
   785→of broken chiral symmetry, the muon as the Goldstino of broken 
   786→sBootstrap SUSY. This is the \emph{exact seed}: the $B$-meson 
   787→direction sits at the flat direction ($\zch = 0$), and the 
   788→$\pi$--$\mu$ pair is massless. Turning on quark masses ($m_q \neq 0$) 
   789→does three things simultaneously:
   790→\begin{enumerate}
   791→\item Gives the pion its GOR mass (lifting the flat direction for $\pi$).
   792→\item Gives the muon its mass (the pseudo-Goldstino acquires mass 
   793→when the explicit breaking parameter $m_q$ is nonzero).
   794→\item Generates the $B$-meson mass (the flat direction for $\Phi_0$ 
   795→is lifted by the Coleman--Weinberg potential, i.e., by the same 
   796→quark masses that feed the GOR formula).
   797→\end{enumerate}
   798→The splitting between (1) and (2) is $f_\pi^2$---the VA relation. 
   799→The seed-to-bloom transition of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples} is the same 
   800→physics seen from the charge-space perspective: $m_q \neq 0$ lifts 
   801→the zero, generates the $b$/$B$ mass, flips the sign of $\zch_s$ 
   802→and $\zch_\pi$, and produces the full triple. The VA equation 
   803→\eqref{eq:pimu} is not one fact among many; it is the generating 
   804→relation from which the bloom follows.
   805→
   806→A note on quantum numbers: the Goldstino of spontaneously broken 
   807→spacetime SUSY is a neutral fermion (the fermionic component of the 
   808→superfield whose $F$-term breaks SUSY). The muon carries electric 
   809→charge and lepton number. The sBootstrap SUSY, however, is not a 
   810→spacetime symmetry but an emergent hadronic symmetry---a 
   811→Miyazawa-type superalgebra \cite{Miyazawa} acting on composite 
   812→states. In this context, the ``Goldstino'' need not carry the 
   813→quantum numbers of a spacetime supercharge; it carries the quantum 
   814→numbers appropriate to the broken hadronic generator. The muon's 
   815→charge is inherited from the pion multiplet it partners.
   816→
   817→\subsection{D-term breaking: electromagnetic splittings}
   818→\label{subsec:dterm}
   819→
   820→The unbroken $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ gauge symmetry allows a 
   821→Fayet--Iliopoulos (FI) term \cite{FayetIliopoulos}:
   822→\begin{equation}
   823→\mathcal{L}_{\text{FI}} = \xi\,D_{\text{em}}\,,
   824→\end{equation}
   825→which shifts scalar masses by $\delta m^2 = Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi$, 
   826→where $Q_{\text{em}}$ is the electric charge. This is the 
   827→supersymmetric version of the electromagnetic mass difference.
   828→
   829→In QCD without SUSY, the electromagnetic mass splitting of the 
   830→pion is given by the Das--Guralnik--Mathur--Low--Young (DGMLY) 
   831→formula \cite{DGMLY1967}:
   832→\begin{equation}
   833→m_{\pi^\pm}^2 - m_{\pi^0}^2 = \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,
   834→\frac{m_\rho^2}{f_\pi^2}\,f_\pi^2 
   835→\approx (35.5\MeV)^2\,,
   836→\end{equation}
   837→which in the SUSY framework becomes a D-term contribution:
   838→\begin{equation}
   839→\xi \sim \frac{3\alpha_{\text{em}}}{4\pi}\,m_\rho^2 
   840→\sim (35\MeV)^2\,.
   841→\end{equation}
   842→
   843→In charge space, the D-term shifts $\zch$ by an amount proportional 
   844→to $Q_{\text{em}}$:
   845→\begin{equation}
   846→\zch \to \zch + \delta\zch\,, \qquad 
   847→\delta\zch \approx \frac{Q_{\text{em}}\,\xi}{2\zch}\,.
   848→\end{equation}
   849→For the pion ($Q_{\text{em}} = \pm 1$, $\zch \approx 11.8$): 
   850→$\delta\zch \approx 0.2\MeV^{1/2}$, matching the observed 
   851→$\zch_{\pi^\pm} - \zch_{\pi^0} = 11.82 - 11.62 = 0.20\MeV^{1/2}$.
   852→
   853→The D-term effect is subleading to the F-term: 
   854→$\xi \sim (35\MeV)^2$ versus $f_\pi^2 \sim (92\MeV)^2$. It 
   855→operates \emph{within} each supermultiplet entry, splitting charged 
   856→from neutral mesons, while the F-term splits bosons from fermions 
   857→across the supermultiplet.
   858→
   859→Witten's inequality \cite{Witten1983} guarantees 
   860→$m_{\pi^\pm} > m_{\pi^0}$ in QCD with electromagnetism---the 
   861→charged pion is always heavier. In the SUSY framework this 
   862→becomes the statement that the D-term contribution is positive 
   863→for $Q_{\text{em}} \neq 0$, consistent with the FI sign.
   864→
   865→\subsection{Mapping the breaking to the tuples}
   866→\label{subsec:mapping}
   867→

867→
   868→The tuple structure maps onto a layered breaking with two independent 
   869→mechanisms (F-term and D-term) plus a separate heavy-sector origin. 
   870→The Volkov--Akulov realization of \S\ref{subsec:va} is not a third 
   871→mechanism: it is the low-energy, composite-level description of the 
   872→same F-term breaking. The VA relation \eqref{eq:pimu} \emph{sets the 
   873→scale} ($f_{\text{VA}} = f_\pi$); the O'Raifeartaigh/SQCD dynamics 
   874→of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} \emph{provides the mechanism} 
   875→($\langle F\rangle \neq 0$).
   876→
   877→\paragraph{Layer 1: F-term/VA (chiral condensate, scale $\sim f_\pi$).}
   878→The chiral condensate $\qqbar \neq 0$ breaks both chiral symmetry 
   879→and the sBootstrap SUSY. This is the primary breaking, affecting 
   880→the light sector. The seed triples $(0, s, c)$ and $(0, \pi, D_s)$ 
   881→are the pre-breaking configurations; the massless member is the flat 
   882→direction whose $F$-term is forced nonzero by confinement. After 
   883→breaking:
   884→\begin{itemize}
   885→\item The zero member is lifted and acquires negative charge 
   886→($\zch < 0$), becoming the sign-flipped member of the full triple 
   887→($-s$, $-\pi$).
   888→\item The full triples $(-s,c,b)$ and $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ emerge, 
   889→incorporating the $b$-quark and $B$-meson sectors.
   890→\item The lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the fermionic shadow: 
   891→the leptons acquire masses through the same condensate, with the 
   892→muon as pseudo-Goldstino.
   893→\end{itemize}
   894→
   895→\paragraph{Layer 2: D-term (electromagnetism, scale $\sim \alpha_{\text{em}} m_\rho$).}
   896→The $U(1)_{\text{em}}$ FI term generates electromagnetic mass 
   897→splittings within each multiplet. This is a perturbative correction 
   898→on top of the F-term breaking, affecting only charged particles. 
   899→It does not create new Koide triples but shifts existing ones by 
   900→$O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$.
   901→
   902→\paragraph{Layer 3: The heavy sector (scale $\sim v_0^{(cbt)}$).}
   903→The $(c,b,t)$ triple at $v_0^2 \approx 29.6\GeV$ operates far 
   904→above the chiral scale. It has no seed (no massless member), no 
   905→meson shadow (top does not hadronize), and its breaking may be 
   906→connected to a different sector of the theory---possibly the 
   907→electroweak Higgs mechanism.
   908→
   909→This layered structure involves three distinct scales, each with 
   910→different physical meaning:
   911→\begin{align}
   912→\text{F-term (boson--fermion splitting):} &\quad 
   913→f_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \quad f_\pi^2 \approx 8{,}500\MeV^2\,; \\
   914→\text{D-term (EM fine structure):} &\quad 
   915→\xi^{1/2} \approx 35\MeV\,, \quad \xi \approx 1{,}200\MeV^2\,; \\
   916→\text{Heavy sector (Koide scale):} &\quad 
   917→v_0^{(cbt)} \approx 172\MeV^{1/2}\,, \quad (v_0^{(cbt)})^2 \approx 29{,}600\MeV\,.
   918→\end{align}
   919→Comparing the squared scales: $f_\pi^2 \gg \xi$ (F-term dominates 
   920→over D-term by a factor $\sim 7$, consistent with the D-term being 
   921→a perturbative $O(\alpha_{\text{em}})$ correction). The heavy-sector 
   922→scale $(v_0^{(cbt)})^2$ carries dimensions of $\MeV$ (not $\MeV^2$) 
   923→because $v_0$ is a charge ($\MeV^{1/2}$), not an energy; direct 
   924→comparison with $f_\pi$ requires specifying which physical quantity 
   925→is being compared.
   926→
   927→\subsection{The O'Raifeartaigh structure in SQCD}
   928→
   929→In Seiberg's SQCD with $N_f = N_c = 3$, the quantum constraint 
   930→\eqref{eq:seiberg} provides a natural setting for the 
   931→O'Raifeartaigh mechanism. The constraint $\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ 
   932→means the moduli space has a specific geometry: not all field directions 
   933→are flat. When quark masses are added via a tree-level superpotential 
   934→$W_{\text{tree}} = m_i M^i{}_i$ (sum over flavors), the $F$-terms become:
   935→\begin{equation}
   936→F_{M^i{}_j} = m_i\,\delta^i_j + \Lambda^6\,(\text{constraint terms})\,.
   937→\end{equation}
   938→
   939→For the seed triple $(0, s, c)$, the ``$0$'' member corresponds to a 
   940→direction in the meson matrix where the tree-level mass vanishes---the 
   941→entry involving the lightest quark combination. In the exact chiral 
   942→limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$), this entry is truly massless, and the 
   943→constraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} forces $\langle F\rangle \neq 0$ for 
   944→that entry. This is the dynamical O'Raifeartaigh mechanism: 
   945→confinement (encoded in $\Lambda^6$) plays the role of the parameter 
   946→$f$ in the superpotential, and the impossibility of setting all 
   947→$F$-terms to zero is a consequence of the strong dynamics rather 
   948→than a choice of superpotential.
   949→
   950→The ISS mechanism (Intriligator--Seiberg--Shih \cite{ISS2006}) 
   951→establishes metastable SUSY-breaking vacua for $N_c < N_f < 3N_c/2$, 
   952→where the rank condition in the magnetic dual prevents all $F$-terms 
   953→from vanishing. The sBootstrap case $N_f = N_c = 3$ is strictly 
   954→\emph{below} the ISS window---at $N_f = N_c$ the low-energy 
   955→description is qualitatively different, governed by the 
   956→quantum-modified constraint \eqref{eq:seiberg} on the moduli space 
   957→rather than by a free magnetic dual. However, the physical QCD has 
   958→$N_f = 3$ light flavors and $N_f = 4$--$5$ if charm and bottom are 
   959→included at their respective thresholds, which does fall within the 
   960→ISS window for $N_c = 3$. The sBootstrap's multi-scale structure---with 
   961→the seed triples involving $s$, $c$, and $b$ quarks at different 
   962→scales---may require treating the effective $N_f$ as 
   963→scale-dependent, entering the ISS regime above the strange threshold.
   964→
   965→\subsection{How the three supermultiplets join}
   966→
   967→The seed-to-bloom transition generates three separate Koide triples. 
   968→But the full sBootstrap requires these triples to be \emph{embedded 
   969→in a single meson matrix}. The $3\times 3$ light-flavor matrix is:
   970→
   971→\begin{equation}
   972→M^{(3)} = \begin{pmatrix}
   973→u\bar{u} & u\bar{d} & u\bar{s} \\
   974→d\bar{u} & d\bar{d} & d\bar{s} \\
   975→s\bar{u} & s\bar{d} & s\bar{s}
   976→\end{pmatrix}
   977→\;\longleftrightarrow\;
   978→\begin{pmatrix}
   979→\pi^0/\eta & \pi^+ & K^+ \\
   980→\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 \\
   981→K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta'
   982→\end{pmatrix}.
   983→\end{equation}
   984→
   985→When charm and bottom are included, the matrix extends to $5\times 5$. 
   986→We display it schematically, with the members of the meson Koide 
   987→triple $(-\pi, D_s, B)$ marked by boxes:
   988→
   989→\begin{equation}
   990→M^{(5)} = \begin{pmatrix}
   991→\pi^0/\eta & \boxed{\pi^+} & K^+ & D^0 & \boxed{B^+} \\
   992→\pi^- & \pi^0/\eta & K^0 & D^- & B^0 \\
   993→K^- & \bar{K}^0 & \eta' & \boxed{D_s^-} & B_s \\
   994→\bar{D}^0 & D^+ & D_s^+ & \eta_c & B_c \\
   995→B^- & \bar{B}^0 & \bar{B}_s & \bar{B}_c & \eta_b
   996→\end{pmatrix},
   997→\end{equation}
   998→where rows are indexed by $(u, d, s, c, b)$ and columns by 
   999→$(\bar{u}, \bar{d}, \bar{s}, \bar{c}, \bar{b})$.
  1000→
  1001→The three members of the meson Koide triple occupy different 
  1002→off-diagonal blocks of this matrix: $\pi^+ = u\bar{d}$ is in the 
  1003→light $(u,d)$ block, $D_s^- = s\bar{c}$ bridges the light-charm 
  1004→sector, and $B^+ = u\bar{b}$ bridges the light-bottom sector. They 
  1005→do not form a ``slice'' in any simple linear-algebraic sense---they 
  1006→are scattered entries connected by the Koide condition on their 
  1007→\emph{masses}, not by their positions in the matrix. The meson seed 
  1008→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ drops the $B^+$ and replaces it with zero (the 
  1009→entry that would involve the top quark, absent from the matrix).
  1010→
  1011→This scattered structure is the central puzzle: the Koide triples 
  1012→are \emph{mass relations} among matrix entries, not submatrices. 
  1013→The question is whether the Seiberg constraint 
  1014→$\det M - B\bar{B} = \Lambda^6$ (appropriately generalized to 
  1015→$5 \times 5$), combined with the SUSY-breaking dynamics, produces 
  1016→correlations among these scattered entries that enforce the 
  1017→energy-balance condition $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$.
  1018→
  1019→\subsection{The energy balance as a fixed point}
  1020→\label{subsec:fixedpoint}
  1021→One possibility is that the energy-balance condition 
  1022→$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is a \emph{dynamical attractor}: 
  1023→any set of three masses evolving under the SUSY-breaking dynamics 
  1024→flows toward the Koide surface $Q = 2/3$, as a fixed point of the 
  1025→RG flow for the soft SUSY-breaking parameters. This is speculative, 
  1026→but the observation that the condition holds for fundamental fermions 
  1027→(leptons, quarks) \emph{and} composite bosons (mesons) across very 
  1028→different scales lends it plausibility. A concrete test would be to 
  1029→show that the one-loop soft-mass RG equations in $\mathcal{N}=1$ 
  1030→SQCD with appropriate boundary conditions flow toward 
  1031→$\sigma(\zch)/\bar{\zch} = 1$; this calculation has not yet been 
  1032→performed.
  1033→
  1034→\subsection{The exact seed and what breaks it}
  1035→\label{subsec:exactseed}
  1036→
  1037→The seed configuration $(0, \zch_a, \zch_b)$ requires \emph{two} 
  1038→independent conditions:
  1039→\begin{enumerate}
  1040→\item One member at $\zch = 0$ exactly (the flat direction).
  1041→\item The ratio of the massive members equals $2 - \sqrt{3}$:
  1042→\begin{equation}
  1043→\label{eq:seedratio}
  1044→\frac{\sqrt{m_a}}{\sqrt{m_b}} = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.26795\,.
  1045→\end{equation}
  1046→\end{enumerate}
  1047→These are logically distinct. The chiral limit ($m_u = m_d = 0$, 
  1048→$\qqbar \neq 0$) enforces condition (1) for the meson sector: the 
  1049→pion is an exact Goldstone boson, $m_\pi = 0$, and in the 
  1050→picture of \S\ref{subsec:fterm} the $B$-meson direction sits at 
  1051→the flat direction before acquiring mass. But the chiral limit says 
  1052→\emph{nothing} about condition (2)---it does not constrain 
  1053→$\sqrt{m_{D_s}}/\sqrt{m_B}$ or $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$ 
  1054→to equal $2 - \sqrt{3}$.
  1055→
  1056→The physical deviations from the exact ratio are small but 
  1057→measurable:
  1058→
  1059→\begin{center}
  1060→\begin{tabular}{lccc}
  1061→\toprule
  1062→Seed & Exact ratio & Physical ratio & Deviation \\
  1063→\midrule
  1064→$(0, \pi, D_s)$ & 0.2680 & 
  1065→$\sqrt{m_\pi}/\sqrt{m_{D_s}} = 0.2663$ & $-0.6\%$ \\
  1066→$(0, s, c)$ & 0.2680 & 
  1067→$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} = 0.2712$ & $+1.2\%$ \\
  1068→$(0 \approx e, \mu, \tau)$ & 0.2680 & 
  1069→$\sqrt{m_\mu}/\sqrt{m_\tau} = 0.2439$ & $-9.0\%$ \\
  1070→\bottomrule
  1071→\end{tabular}
  1072→\end{center}
  1073→
  1074→The meson seed is closest to the exact ratio (0.6\% deviation). 
  1075→The quark seed deviates by 1.2\%. A natural thought is that QCD 
  1076→running might shift the quark ratio: perhaps at some scale $\mu^*$ 
  1077→the ratio $\sqrt{m_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{m_c(\mu^*)}$ hits $2 - \sqrt{3}$ 
  1078→exactly. But this is \emph{not possible within QCD alone}. At leading 
  1079→order, all quark masses run with the same anomalous dimension 
  1080→$\gamma_m$:
  1081→\begin{equation}
  1082→m_i(\mu) = m_i(\mu_0)\left(\frac{\alpha_s(\mu)}{\alpha_s(\mu_0)}\right)^{\gamma_m/\beta_0},
  1083→\end{equation}
  1084→so the ratio $m_s(\mu)/m_c(\mu)$---and therefore 
  1085→$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}$---is an RG invariant of QCD. Higher-order 
  1086→corrections break this invariance, but only at the $O(\alpha_s^2)$ 
  1087→level, far too small to account for 1.2\%.
  1088→
  1089→The quark seed deviation is therefore \emph{intrinsic}: the 
  1090→$\overline{\text{MS}}$ mass ratio $m_s/m_c$ is a fixed number 
  1091→(at any QCD scale), and it misses the Koide target by 1.2\%. 
  1092→Three possible explanations present themselves:
  1093→
  1094→\paragraph{Electroweak running.} Above the electroweak scale, 
  1095→quark masses are controlled by Yukawa couplings $y_i$ to the 
  1096→Higgs, which \emph{do} run differently for different flavors: 
  1097→the $\beta$-functions for $y_s$ and $y_c$ involve different 
  1098→electroweak quantum numbers ($SU(2)_L$ doublet structure, 
  1099→hypercharge). The ratio $y_s/y_c$ evolves with scale, and 
  1100→there may exist a scale $\mu^*$ above the electroweak 
  1101→threshold where $\sqrt{y_s(\mu^*)}/\sqrt{y_c(\mu^*)} = 2 - \sqrt{3}$. 
  1102→This would identify the ``seed scale'' as an electroweak-sector 
  1103→phenomenon, not a QCD one---consistent with the sBootstrap's 
  1104→identification of the breaking with $SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ 
  1105→rather than $SU(3)_c$.
  1106→
  1107→\paragraph{Charge running.} Even if mass ratios are QCD-invariant, 
  1108→the Koide decomposition $\zch_k = z_0 + z_k$ may have its own 
  1109→flow. The vacuum energy $v_0 = z_0^2$ sets the common scale, 
  1110→and the fluctuations $z_k$ encode the deviations. If the 
  1111→energy-balance condition $\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ is a 
  1112→dynamical attractor (as suggested in \S\ref{subsec:fixedpoint}), 
  1113→the $z_k$ may evolve under a flow in which the \emph{ratio} 
  1114→$z_a/z_b$ approaches $2 - \sqrt{3}$ at the fixed point while 
  1115→the individual masses (and their QCD-invariant ratios) remain 
  1116→unchanged. In this picture, the 1.2\% deviation measures how 
  1117→far the quark sector is from the attractor.
  1118→
  1119→\paragraph{Meson vs.\ quark deviations.} The meson deviation 
  1120→(0.6\%) is smaller than the quark deviation (1.2\%), and for 
  1121→a different reason: meson masses are \emph{not} simple functions 
  1122→of quark mass ratios. The pion mass is given by the GOR 
  1123→relation $m_\pi^2 f_\pi^2 = -(m_u + m_d)\qqbar$; the $D_s$ 
  1124→mass involves heavy-quark effective theory; and both receive 
  1125→chiral perturbation theory corrections. These nonperturbative 
  1126→QCD effects are the dominant source of the meson seed deviation, 
  1127→and are in principle calculable at NLO in ChPT.
  1128→
  1129→\paragraph{A remarkable feature of the VA relation.} The 
  1130→foundational equation $m_\pi^2 - m_\mu^2 = f_\pi^2$ contains 
  1131→no QCD coupling constant: no $\alpha_s$, no $N_c$, no 
  1132→$\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$. It is a pure mass relation among three 
  1133→experimentally measured quantities---two masses and a decay 
  1134→constant---holding to 2.2\%. In the sBootstrap, this is natural: 
  1135→the relation is a SUSY mass splitting, set by the breaking 
  1136→scale $f_\pi$, and SUSY mass relations do not involve the gauge 
  1137→coupling of the confining theory. Standard QCD \emph{explains} 
  1138→$f_\pi$ in terms of $\qqbar$ and $\alpha_s$; the sBootstrap 
  1139→\emph{uses} $f_\pi$ as the fundamental parameter, with QCD 
  1140→providing the dynamical origin but not appearing in the mass 
  1141→formula. The absence of QCD factors in \eqref{eq:pimu} is a 
  1142→hint that the relevant symmetry is not $SU(3)_c$ but the 
  1143→hadronic superalgebra that connects pions to leptons.
  1144→
  1145→The lepton ``seed'' has the worst ratio match ($-9\%$), which 
  1146→is consistent with the observation that the lepton triple is 
  1147→\emph{not} a true seed: $m_e \neq 0$, and the electron was never 
  1148→at the flat direction. The leptons are an ``almost-seed'' whose 
  1149→excellent $Q = 0.66666$ arises not from the seed structure but 
  1150→from the smallness of $m_e$, which makes the two conditions 
  1151→approximately degenerate. For the leptons, condition (1) is 
  1152→approximate rather than exact, and condition (2) is correspondingly 
  1153→relaxed.
  1154→
  1155→\subsection{What the $(c,b,t)$ triple tells us}
  1156→
  1157→The $(c,b,t)$ triple ($Q = 0.6695$, $v_0^2 = 29.6\GeV$) has 
  1158→neither a seed nor a meson shadow. It operates at a scale far 
  1159→above $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}$, suggesting its origin is different:
  1160→
  1161→\begin{enumerate}
  1162→\item It may be governed by the electroweak Higgs mechanism rather 
  1163→than the chiral condensate. The top Yukawa $y_t \approx 1$ is 
  1164→``natural'' in the sense that $m_t \approx v_{\text{EW}}/\sqrt{2}$; 
  1165→the Koide condition on $(c,b,t)$ would then constrain the Yukawa 
  1166→coupling matrix.
  1167→\item It may represent a Volkov--Akulov realization at the 
  1168→electroweak scale: if there is a second SUSY breaking at 
  1169→$f' \sim v_{\text{EW}}$, the $(c,b,t)$ triple would be its 
  1170→analog of the $(\pi, \mu)$ system, but with $f'$ replacing $f_\pi$.
  1171→\item Or it may be a purely kinematic accident---the quark masses 
  1172→happen to satisfy the Koide condition without a dynamical reason. 
  1173→The 0.42\% precision makes this unlikely but not impossible.
  1174→\end{enumerate}
  1175→
  1176→Interpretation (1) is most natural within the sBootstrap: the 
  1177→$(c,b,t)$ triple involves quarks whose masses are dominated by 
  1178→Yukawa couplings to the Higgs, not by the chiral condensate, and the 
  1179→Koide condition would then be a constraint on the Yukawa matrix 
  1180→rather than on the SQCD dynamics. The chiral sector and the 
  1181→electroweak sector would each independently produce a Koide-type 
  1182→energy balance, with the charm and bottom quarks serving as the 
  1183→bridge between the two.
  1184→
  1185→If interpretation (2) is also invoked, the sBootstrap has two breaking 
  1186→scales:
  1187→\begin{equation}
  1188→f_{\text{chiral}} = f_\pi \approx 92\MeV\,, \qquad
  1189→f_{\text{EW}} \sim v_{\text{EW}} \approx 246\GeV\,,
  1190→\end{equation}
  1191→and the full spectrum requires both. The light sector (leptons, 
  1192→light mesons) is governed by $f_\pi$; the heavy sector (top, and 
  1193→by extension charm and bottom through the Koide network) is 
  1194→governed by $v_{\text{EW}}$. The charm and bottom quarks, 
  1195→appearing in both the $(0,s,c)/(-s,c,b)$ light-sector triples 
  1196→and the $(c,b,t)$ heavy-sector triple, are the ``bridges'' 
  1197→between the two scales.
  1198→
  1199→%======================================================================
  1200→\section{The Electron Mass and the Lepton Seed}
  1201→\label{sec:electron}
  1202→%======================================================================
  1203→
  1204→The charged lepton triple $(e, \mu, \tau)$ is the ``almost-seed'': 
  1205→$\zch_e = 0.715\MeV^{1/2}$ is small but nonzero. The electron's 
  1206→charge is near zero because of cancellation in $\zch_e = z_0 + z_e$:

\section{Mass Relations and Iterative Chains}
\label{sec:mass_chains}
%======================================================================

The preceding sections established the Koide energy-balance condition
$Q = 2/3$ and identified the triples that satisfy it. In this section
we quantify how special the lepton triple really is, introduce the
angular parametrization that reveals a hidden rational structure in
the Koide phase, and trace an iterative chain that connects the heavy
quarks to the strange sector. We report both successes and failures:
the scaling rule that generates the quark triple from the lepton
triple works once and then stops, and the iterative chain stalls at
a mass too light for any known fermion. These negative results
constrain the scope of the pattern.

\subsection{The lepton Koide formula: how special?}
\label{subsec:lepton_koide}

Using PDG~2024 values $m_e = 0.51100\MeV$, $m_\mu = 105.658\MeV$,
$m_\tau = 1776.86 \pm 0.12\MeV$:
\begin{equation}
Q(e,\mu,\tau) = \frac{(\sqrt{m_e} + \sqrt{m_\mu} + \sqrt{m_\tau})^2}
{m_e + m_\mu + m_\tau} = 1.500014 \pm 0.000015\,,
\end{equation}
where we have written $Q$ in the ``sum-of-roots-squared over
sum-of-masses'' convention ($Q = 3/2$ corresponds to the
energy-balance condition $Q_{\text{prev}} = 2/3$ of
\S\ref{sec:koide_algebra}).\footnote{The two conventions are related
by $Q_{\text{here}} = 3\,Q_{\text{prev}}$. The value $3/2$ and the
value $2/3$ encode the same condition; we use $3/2$ in this section
because the literature on the Koide formula most commonly quotes
$Q = 2/3$ with the reciprocal normalization
$Q = \sum m / (\sum\sqrt{m})^2$, and we wish to avoid confusion with
that sign convention. Explicitly:
$Q_{\text{here}} = (\sum\sqrt{m})^2 / \sum m = 3/2$, while the
convention in Sec.~\ref{sec:koide_algebra} is
$Q_{\text{prev}} = \sum m / (\sum\sqrt{m})^2 = 2/3$.}
The deviation from $3/2$ is $|Q - 3/2| = 1.38 \times 10^{-5}$,
corresponding to $0.91\sigma$ given the uncertainty dominated by
$\delta m_\tau$.

\paragraph{Look-elsewhere effect.}
The Standard Model contains 9~charged fermions (3~leptons, 6~quarks).
An exhaustive scan of all $\binom{9}{3} = 84$ mass triplets yields
the ranking in Table~\ref{tab:koide_scan}.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Top-ranked SM triplets by proximity to $Q = 3/2$. The gap
between rank~1 and rank~2 is a factor of~450.}
\label{tab:koide_scan}
\begin{tabular}{clc}
\toprule
Rank & Triplet & $|Q - 3/2|$ \\
\midrule
1 & $(e, \mu, \tau)$ & $1.38 \times 10^{-5}$ \\
2 & $(c, b, t)$ & $6.2 \times 10^{-3}$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

To assess the statistical weight of the lepton result, we performed a
Monte Carlo study: $10{,}000$ random mass sets of 9~fermions drawn
log-uniform on $[0.1, 200{,}000]\MeV$ were scanned for the best
triplet. The fraction with $|Q - 3/2|_{\min} \leq 1.38 \times 10^{-5}$
gives a look-elsewhere-corrected significance of $\mathbf{2.8\sigma}$.
This is suggestive but not conclusive: the lepton Koide formula is
unlikely to be a pure accident, but the evidence does not reach the
discovery threshold.

\subsection{The Koide angle parametrization}
\label{subsec:koide_angle}

Any triple satisfying $Q = 3/2$ exactly can be written
\cite{Koide1983}
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:koide_angle}
\sqrt{m_k} = \sqrt{M_0}\left(1 + \sqrt{2}\cos\!\left(
\frac{2\pi k}{3} + \delta_0\right)\right), \qquad k = 0,1,2\,,
\end{equation}
where $M_0 > 0$ is the scale parameter and $\delta_0 \in [0, 2\pi)$
is the Koide phase. This is not an approximation: any three
non-negative numbers whose $Q$-ratio equals $3/2$ are exactly
parametrized by some $(M_0, \delta_0)$.

Fitting to the charged lepton masses:
\begin{equation}
M_0 = 313.84\MeV\,, \qquad \delta_0 = 2.3166\;\text{rad}\,.
\end{equation}
The scale $M_0 \approx 314\MeV$ is the vacuum energy $v_0^2$ of
\S\ref{sec:seeds}, now identified as the mass parameter of the
angular decomposition.

\paragraph{The phase is close to $2/9$.}
The parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle} has a $2\pi/3$ periodicity
in $\delta_0$ (permuting the three masses). The physically
meaningful quantity is the reduced phase
\begin{equation}
\delta_0 \bmod \frac{2\pi}{3} = 0.22223\;\text{rad}\,.
\end{equation}
This is strikingly close to the fraction $2/9 = 0.22222\ldots$:
\begin{equation}
\delta_0 \bmod \frac{2\pi}{3} - \frac{2}{9} = 7.8 \times 10^{-6}\,,
\end{equation}
a residual of $\mathbf{35}$~\textbf{ppm} relative to $2/9$.
(A figure of 7~ppm has appeared in the literature, but that uses the
unreduced phase $\delta_0$ as the denominator; the relevant
denominator is the value being matched, $2/9$.)

\paragraph{Statistical significance of the rational approximation.}
There are 128~``nice'' fractions $p/q$ with $q \leq 20$ and
$0 < p/q < 2\pi/3$. The probability that a random phase (uniform
on $[0, 2\pi/3)$) falls within $7.8 \times 10^{-6}$ of \emph{any}
of these 128 fractions gives a look-elsewhere-corrected significance
of $\mathbf{3.1\sigma}$. Evaluated specifically against the fraction
$2/9$ alone (i.e., without look-elsewhere correction), the
significance is $\mathbf{4.3\sigma}$. The $2/9$ proximity is the
strongest single numerical coincidence in the Koide system.

\subsection{The scaling rule: $M_1 = 3M_0$, $\delta_1 = 3\delta_0$}
\label{subsec:scaling}

A natural question is whether other Koide triples can be obtained
from the lepton parameters by a simple scaling. The ansatz
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:scaling}
M_1 = 3\,M_0 = 941.5\MeV\,, \qquad
\delta_1 = 3\,\delta_0 = 6.9497\;\text{rad}\,,
\end{equation}
inserted into~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}, predicts a quark triple.
Table~\ref{tab:scaling_pred} compares predictions to PDG values.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Masses predicted by the $\times 3$ scaling rule vs.\ PDG
central values. The quark Koide triple uses a negative sign for
$\sqrt{m_s}$, as in \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.}
\label{tab:scaling_pred}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Quark & Predicted (MeV) & PDG (MeV) & Deviation \\
\midrule
$s$ & 92.3 & 93.4 & $1.2\%$ \\
$c$ & 1360 & 1270 & $7.1\%$ \\
$b$ & 4197 & 4180 & $0.4\%$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The agreement for $s$ and $b$ is at the percent level; charm shows a
larger ($7.1\%$) discrepancy. The predicted triple satisfies the
Koide condition with a negative square root for the strange quark:
\begin{equation}
Q(-\sqrt{m_s},\;\sqrt{m_c},\;\sqrt{m_b})
= \frac{(-\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c} + \sqrt{m_b})^2}{m_s + m_c + m_b}
= \frac{3}{2}\,,
\end{equation}
holding to machine precision (residual $2.2 \times 10^{-16}$), since
the masses are generated from the parametrization~\eqref{eq:koide_angle}
which enforces $Q = 3/2$ identically.

scales: $m_s(2\GeV)$, $m_c(m_c)$, $m_b(m_b)$, and $m_t$ as the pole
mass. The predicted masses emerge at a single internal scale set by
$M_1$. Running all masses to a common $\overline{\text{MS}}$ scale
would change the individual deviations in Table~\ref{tab:scaling_pred}
but not the structural point: the $\times 3$ scaling produces a
recognizable quark triple. As noted in \S\ref{subsec:exactseed},
QCD mass \emph{ratios} are RG invariants at leading order, so the
Koide condition itself is scheme-independent, but the comparison
of absolute masses to PDG values carries an $O(\alpha_s)$ ambiguity.

\subsection{The second scaling fails}
\label{subsec:second_scaling}

Iterating the scaling rule:
\begin{equation}
M_2 = 9\,M_0 = 2825\MeV\,, \qquad
\delta_2 = 9\,\delta_0 = 20.849\;\text{rad}\,,
\end{equation}
gives predicted masses $(478,\; 92.3,\; 16{,}377)\MeV$. Only the
middle value echoes $m_s$. The lightest ($478\MeV$) does not match
any known quark mass, and the heaviest ($16.4\GeV$) falls between
$m_b$ and $m_t$ with no candidate. No SM triplet accommodates these
values.

that constrains any attempt to build a recursive tower of Koide
triples: the multiplicative rule $M \to 3M$, $\delta \to 3\delta$
is not a general principle but a one-off relation between the lepton
and quark sectors. Whether this single success is accidental or
reflects a deeper connection (perhaps related to the three colors
of QCD, or to the three generations) remains open.

\subsection{Iterative chain: descending from $t$ and $b$}
\label{subsec:chain}

An alternative approach to connecting the quark masses is purely
algebraic: given two known masses, solve the Koide equation
$Q = 3/2$ for the third. Starting from the top and bottom quarks:

\paragraph{Step~1: $Q(m_b, m_?, m_t) = 3/2$.}
The Koide equation with $(m_b, m_t)$ known is quadratic in
$\sqrt{m_?}$, yielding four branches (two signs for $\sqrt{m_?}$,
two roots of each quadratic). The solutions are:
\begin{equation}
m_? = \{1357,\; 60{,}280,\; 1.34 \times 10^6,\;
3.55 \times 10^6\}\MeV\,.
\end{equation}
Only the first branch gives a physically viable mass:
$m_? = 1357\MeV$, which we identify with the charm quark
(PDG: $m_c(m_c) = 1270\MeV$, deviation $6.8\%$).

\paragraph{Step~2: $Q(m_c, m_?, m_b) = 3/2$.}
With $m_c = 1357\MeV$ (the chain value, not the PDG value) and
$m_b = 4180\MeV$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 92.17\MeV\,,
\end{equation}
which we identify with the strange quark. The negative square root
emerges naturally from the algebra---the solution selects the branch
with $\sqrt{m_?} < 0$ in the signed-charge framework, consistent
with the $(-s, c, b)$ structure of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.

\paragraph{Step~3: the chain stalls.}
Continuing from $(m_s, m_c)$ with $m_s = 92.17\MeV$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 0.035\MeV\,.
\end{equation}
This is far too light for either $u$ ($2.16\MeV$) or $d$
($4.67\MeV$). The chain does not reach the first-generation quarks;
it stalls at a mass two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.

\paragraph{Step~4: cyclic echo.}
The stalled mass provides a striking algebraic consistency check.
Taking $m_{\text{stall}} = 0.035\MeV$ and $m_s = 92.17\MeV$, and
solving $Q(m_{\text{stall}}, m_?, m_s) = 3/2$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 1357\MeV \quad \text{(to machine epsilon)}\,.
\end{equation}
The chain returns to its starting point: $t \to c \to s \to
\text{stall} \to c \to \cdots$. This is not a numerical coincidence
but an algebraic necessity: the Koide condition, combined with the
specific masses of $b$ and $t$, defines a closed orbit in
$(m_1, m_2, m_3)$ space. The cycle is:
\begin{equation}
\cdots \;\longrightarrow\; (b,t) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
\;\longrightarrow\; (c,b) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} s
\;\longrightarrow\; (s,c) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} \text{stall}
\;\longrightarrow\; (\text{stall}, s) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
\;\longrightarrow\; \cdots
\end{equation}

The chain captures $c$, $b$, $s$ but misses $u$, $d$, and the
leptons entirely. The first generation is outside the algebraic
orbit seeded by the third generation.

\subsection{Cross-checks and master comparison}
\label{subsec:cross_checks}

Two independent routes lead to the quark masses: the
lepton-seeded scaling rule (\S\ref{subsec:scaling}) and the
top-bottom iterative chain (\S\ref{subsec:chain}).
Table~\ref{tab:master} compares all predictions.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Master comparison of predicted quark masses (MeV). Chain~A
descends from $(t,b)$ via the Koide equation. Chain~B applies
the $\times 3$ scaling to the lepton triple. The small discrepancy
between the two ($1357$ vs.\ $1360\MeV$ for charm) arises from
different inputs: Chain~A uses the PDG $m_t$ and $m_b$ directly,
while Chain~B uses the fitted $(M_0, \delta_0)$ from leptons.}
\label{tab:master}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Quark & Chain A & Chain B & PDG & Status \\
 & (from $t,b$) & (from leptons) & & \\
\midrule
$s$ & 92.2 & 92.3 & 93.4 & $1\text{--}1.2\%$ deviation \\
$c$ & 1357 & 1360 & 1270 & $7\text{--}7.1\%$ deviation \\
$b$ & (input) & 4197 & 4180 & $0.4\%$ deviation \\
$t$ & (input) & --- & 172\,760 & --- \\
stall & 0.035 & --- & --- & no SM match \\
$M_2$ set & --- & $(478,\,92,\,16377)$ & --- & no SM match \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The convergence of the two chains on $m_s \approx 92\MeV$ and
$m_c \approx 1357\text{--}1360\MeV$ is the central positive result.
Both routes, starting from completely different inputs (lepton masses
vs.\ heavy quark masses), produce the same strange and charm masses
to within $0.2\%$ of each other. Against PDG values, the strange
quark prediction is excellent ($\sim 1\%$), and the bottom quark
prediction is even better ($0.4\%$); the charm prediction shows a
persistent $\sim 7\%$ overshoot.

The negative results are equally informative:
\begin{itemize}
\item The $\times 3$ scaling does not iterate: the $\times 9$ step
produces no viable triple.
\item The iterative chain does not reach the first generation: it
stalls at $0.035\MeV$, two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.
\item The chain is algebraically cyclic: it loops through
$(c, s, \text{stall})$ indefinitely, never escaping to new masses.
\end{itemize}
These failures delineate the boundary of the Koide pattern: it
connects the second and third quark generations (and, via the
scaling rule, the leptons), but does not extend to the first
generation or beyond the Standard Model spectrum. Whatever
dynamics underlies the energy-balance condition
$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ operates within a limited domain.

$m_? = 1357\MeV$, which we identify with the charm quark
(PDG: $m_c(m_c) = 1270\MeV$, deviation $6.8\%$).

\paragraph{Step~2: $Q(m_c, m_?, m_b) = 3/2$.}
With $m_c = 1357\MeV$ (the chain value, not the PDG value) and
$m_b = 4180\MeV$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 92.17\MeV\,,
\end{equation}
which we identify with the strange quark. The negative square root
emerges naturally from the algebra---the solution selects the branch
with $\sqrt{m_?} < 0$ in the signed-charge framework, consistent
with the $(-s, c, b)$ structure of \S\ref{sec:two_tuples}.

\paragraph{Step~3: the chain stalls.}
Continuing from $(m_s, m_c)$ with $m_s = 92.17\MeV$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 0.035\MeV\,.
\end{equation}
This is far too light for either $u$ ($2.16\MeV$) or $d$
($4.67\MeV$). The chain does not reach the first-generation quarks;
it stalls at a mass two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.

\paragraph{Step~4: cyclic echo.}
The stalled mass provides a striking algebraic consistency check.
Taking $m_{\text{stall}} = 0.035\MeV$ and $m_s = 92.17\MeV$, and
solving $Q(m_{\text{stall}}, m_?, m_s) = 3/2$:
\begin{equation}
m_? = 1357\MeV \quad \text{(to machine epsilon)}\,.
\end{equation}
The chain returns to its starting point: $t \to c \to s \to
\text{stall} \to c \to \cdots$. This is not a numerical coincidence
but an algebraic necessity: the Koide condition, combined with the
specific masses of $b$ and $t$, defines a closed orbit in
$(m_1, m_2, m_3)$ space. The cycle is:
\begin{equation}
\cdots \;\longrightarrow\; (b,t) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
\;\longrightarrow\; (c,b) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} s
\;\longrightarrow\; (s,c) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} \text{stall}
\;\longrightarrow\; (\text{stall}, s) \xrightarrow{Q=3/2} c
\;\longrightarrow\; \cdots
\end{equation}

The chain captures $c$, $b$, $s$ but misses $u$, $d$, and the
leptons entirely. The first generation is outside the algebraic
orbit seeded by the third generation.

\subsection{Cross-checks and master comparison}
\label{subsec:cross_checks}

Two independent routes lead to the quark masses: the
lepton-seeded scaling rule (\S\ref{subsec:scaling}) and the
top-bottom iterative chain (\S\ref{subsec:chain}).
Table~\ref{tab:master} compares all predictions.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Master comparison of predicted quark masses (MeV). Chain~A
descends from $(t,b)$ via the Koide equation. Chain~B applies
the $\times 3$ scaling to the lepton triple. The small discrepancy
between the two ($1357$ vs.\ $1360\MeV$ for charm) arises from
different inputs: Chain~A uses the PDG $m_t$ and $m_b$ directly,
while Chain~B uses the fitted $(M_0, \delta_0)$ from leptons.}
\label{tab:master}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Quark & Chain A & Chain B & PDG & Status \\
 & (from $t,b$) & (from leptons) & & \\
\midrule
$s$ & 92.2 & 92.3 & 93.4 & $1\text{--}1.2\%$ deviation \\
$c$ & 1357 & 1360 & 1270 & $7\text{--}7.1\%$ deviation \\
$b$ & (input) & 4197 & 4180 & $0.4\%$ deviation \\
$t$ & (input) & --- & 172\,760 & --- \\
stall & 0.035 & --- & --- & no SM match \\
$M_2$ set & --- & $(478,\,92,\,16377)$ & --- & no SM match \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The convergence of the two chains on $m_s \approx 92\MeV$ and
$m_c \approx 1357\text{--}1360\MeV$ is the central positive result.
Both routes, starting from completely different inputs (lepton masses
vs.\ heavy quark masses), produce the same strange and charm masses
to within $0.2\%$ of each other. Against PDG values, the strange
quark prediction is excellent ($\sim 1\%$), and the bottom quark
prediction is even better ($0.4\%$); the charm prediction shows a
persistent $\sim 7\%$ overshoot.

The negative results are equally informative:
\begin{itemize}
\item The $\times 3$ scaling does not iterate: the $\times 9$ step
produces no viable triple.
\item The iterative chain does not reach the first generation: it
stalls at $0.035\MeV$, two orders of magnitude below $m_u$.
\item The chain is algebraically cyclic: it loops through
$(c, s, \text{stall})$ indefinitely, never escaping to new masses.
\end{itemize}
These failures delineate the boundary of the Koide pattern: it
connects the second and third quark generations (and, via the
scaling rule, the leptons), but does not extend to the first
generation or beyond the Standard Model spectrum. Whatever
dynamics underlies the energy-balance condition
$\langle z_k^2\rangle = z_0^2$ operates within a limited domain.
